summary: I am originally from Iran, where I lived until about 26 years ago before my family and I decided to leave. The political situation changed drastically after the Shah was overthrown by a religious leader, and the new Islamic laws made life very difficult, especially for women like me. Despite the challenges, I always tried to make the best of what I had, but the corrupt government system limited my opportunities and freedoms. Leaving Iran was hard because I had to leave friends and family behind, but I knew that escaping was necessary for a better future.

The Shah of Iran was the king who ruled before the religious leader took over and changed the government system. I remember when the Shah was rolled out, it marked a turning point for my family and me because the new regime imposed strict Islamic laws that affected everyone’s lives. This political upheaval forced us to seek a way out of Iran to escape the unfair system. The Shah's removal was the beginning of a difficult journey for us, but it was also the reason we eventually left our homeland.

The United States of America became my new home after my family and I left Iran. My parents were the first to get visas here, and after they settled, they helped me and my siblings get green cards. When I arrived, I didn’t speak any English, but I took classes and made friends with other immigrants who were very kind and supportive. Living in America gave me freedoms and opportunities I never had in Iran, like the right to an education and the chance to build a family on my own terms. I’m very grateful for the life I have here, and I feel proud to raise my children in this land of opportunity.

Before I could come to the United States, I had to spend about eight months in Pakistan, which was a very difficult time for me. The conditions were harsh; we didn’t have clean water, good food, or even a fridge, but we managed to survive there while waiting for our visas. Karachi was the city where I stayed during this period, and although it was tough, I held on to hope that I would soon join my family in America. That time in Pakistan was a test of endurance, but it was a necessary step in my journey to a better life.

Karachi, Pakistan, was where I stayed for about eight months before finally getting my visa to the United States. Life there was very challenging; we lacked basic necessities like clean water and good food, and the living conditions were far from comfortable. Despite these hardships, Karachi was a crucial stop on my path to America, and I was determined to make it through so I could reunite with my family. Looking back, those months in Karachi tested my resilience but also strengthened my hope for a brighter future.
